macro_segments.title: Opening remarks on the daf and review of pigul thought with half-measures

micro_segments.title: Introduction to daf 13 and the mishna on eating and burning half-measures
Discussion of whether eating and burning can combine as pigul
Gemara's distinction: eating-eating vs. eating-burning; identifying the tanna of the mishna as Rabbi Eliezer

macro_segments.title: Abye's reinterpretation and the explicit versus implicit lessons of the mishna

micro_segments.title: Abaye's rejection of the inference about eating the kometz and restatement of the mishna's plain meaning
Citation of previous mishna showing eating and eating of different types combine despite being distinct invalidities
Gemara's resolution: the mishna teaches the simple halacha that eating and burning do not combine

macro_segments.title: Transition and the Rashbam's explanatory note on the previous sugya

micro_segments.title: Brief explanation of the Rashbam's inclusion and his revision of the previous sugya

macro_segments.title: New mishna: pigul thought on levona and Rabbi Yose's limitation of pigul to the matir

micro_segments.title: Mishna case of pigul thought on levona; Rabbi Yose's position that it does not constitute pigul
Reish Lakish's explanation: pigul applies only to matir, not to another matir
Application to two bowls of levona for lechem hapanim and Gemara's challenge to this reading

macro_segments.title: Gemara's reinterpretation of Rabbi Yose's reason: dependency vs. physical distinctness

micro_segments.title: Gemara rejects inference that mishna implies eating kometz combines; clarifies Rabbi Yose's actual reason
Explanation of einah be'ikuv mincha: levona is not held back like kometz holds back shirayim
Illustration of order-independence: either kometz or levona can be burned first

macro_segments.title: Rabbis' response: levona is physically placed in same vessel and becomes one conceptually with mincha

micro_segments.title: Rabbis agree on principle but disagree on application; levona placed on mincha in same vessel makes them one unit

macro_segments.title: Digression on aron levona: whether gathering levona by a non-kohen invalidates the mincha

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yanai's position that gathering levona by a non-kohen is invalid
Rav Yirmiyah's explanation: gathering is a form of havicha (conveyance), requiring a kohen

macro_segments.title: Analysis of the four primary avodot of the mincha and necessity as a principle for defining avodot

micro_segments.title: Gemara cites the four avodot: kometz, noten bikli, molich, and maktir
Question on noten bikli: is it an avodah because it parallels kabbala or because it is necessary?
Principle debated: does necessity alone make something an avodah, or must it fit defined categories?

macro_segments.title: Continuation: Gemara's resolution that noten bikli parallels kabbala, rejecting necessity alone

micro_segments.title: Gemara asserts noten bikli is truly like kabbala despite differences in execution
Both kabbala and noten bikli achieve the same result of getting item into vessel; only method differs

macro_segments.title: New mishna: pigul thought on one of two lambs or loaves for shtei halechem

micro_segments.title: Mishna: slaughter two lambs with intent to eat one loaf of shtei halechem tomorrow
Rabbi Yose: only the specific loaf you thought about becomes pigul; the other is invalid but not pigul
Rabbis: both loaves become pigul regardless of which you had thought about

macro_segments.title: Gemara's analysis of Rabbi Yose's principle: pigul applies only to what you specifically intended

micro_segments.title: Rav Huna's explanation: Rabbi Yose applies pigul only to the specific object of thought, not adjacent parts
Logical justification: thought is not superior to action; if action on right thigh doesn't affect left thigh, neither should thought
Textual support: Torah says vehanefesh ha'ochelet mimenu—from that which you thought about specifically

macro_segments.title: Challenge from baraita: pigul requires thought of both loaves with combined kezayit

micro_segments.title: Baraita states pigul requires intention to eat both loaves with a combined kezayit
Identification of tanna: this baraita reflects Rabbi Yose's view, not the rabbis'

macro_segments.title: Final resolution: Rabbi Yose's position depends on whether objects are one unit or distinct

micro_segments.title: If two loaves are considered one unit, thought of combined kezayit can make both pigul
If everything is intrinsically distinct, even half-kezayit measures cannot combine

topical_tags.term: mefagel et hamatir (one matir creating pigul in another)
four avodot (kometz, noten bikli, molich, maktir)
